revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Agatanushi]][[en:Agatanushi]][[es:Agatanushi]][[ja:県主]][[mr:आगतनुशी]][[pt:Agatanushi]][[vi:Agatanushi]][[zh:縣主 (日本)]]
{{Italic title|reason=|qq=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Agatanushi'''''|県主|qq=}} was the name of an ancient [[:en:Title of nobility|Title of nobility]] in the ''[[kabane]]'' system of {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}} Japan from the 4th through 6th century AD, before the introduction of the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The word is a combination of the ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' for {{nihongo|''nushi''|主|chief|qq=}} with {{nihongo|''Agata''|県|||qq=}}, a political unit smaller than a province, and the ''agatanushi'' Ranked below the title of {{nihongo|''kuni no miyatsuko''|国造||qq=}}. It is thought that the ''agatanushi'' were originally chieftains of small [[Kofun period]] tribal states which had been annexed by the [[:en:Yamato state|Yamato state]]. <ref name=Japan>{{cite book |last1=Campbell |first1=Allen |last2=Nobel |first2=David S |title=Japan: An Illustrated Encyclopedia |publisher=Kodansha |date=1993 |page=13 |isbn=406205938X|qq=}}</ref> Per the Chinese “History of the Sui Dynasty”, (589-618), Yamato was divided into ''kuni'' (国 provinces), which were subdivided into ''agata'' (県), which were governed respectively by ''kuni no miyatsuko'' and ''agatanushi'', who were responsible for collecting tribute. However, these offices seem to have been a confirmation of de facto local power, rather than a granting of office by the Yamato Court. ==References== {{reflist|qq=}}
[[en:Agatanushi]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Agatanushi]] [[es:Agatanushi]] [[ja:県主]] [[mr:आगतनुशी]] [[pt:Agatanushi]] [[vi:Agatanushi]] [[zh:縣主 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q10746487}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:県主]]
